content.body: annevk: for https://github.com/whatwg/infra/pull/709 (using infra for sanitizer), do we need infra to allow embedders to define an "equality" operator and how all the algorithms like "contains" use it? or is it enough that the sanitizer section says something to the effect of "items in this lists are considered equal when..."
content.formatted_body: annevk: for https://github.com/whatwg/infra/pull/709 (using infra for sanitizer), do we need infra to allow embedders to define an "equality" operator and how all the algorithms like "contains" use it? or is it enough that the sanitizer section says something to the effect of "items in this lists are considered equal when..."

content.body: Noam Rosenthal: there is an open issue on equality we need to get to at some point. I think I would accept Sanitizer without that getting defined, but we should note it in the HTML source that it needs to be done.
content.formatted_body: Noam Rosenthal: there is an open issue on equality we need to get to at some point. I think I would accept Sanitizer without that getting defined, but we should note it in the HTML source that it needs to be done.

content.body: Open issue against Infra that is.
SG, I'll link the open issue from a note
I guess zcorpan also needs to say if that's okay.
content.formatted_body: I guess zcorpan also needs to say if that's okay.

content.body: I don't think we want a note. Either XXX or a source comment.
Yea I meant one of these things
Maybe I'll get a chance to send an infra PR for equality operators at some point (without blocking sanitizer work)
Sounds good

content.body:
hi, some of you are coming to the Web Engines Hackfest in June 15-17, so we were thinking if it'd be a good idea to arrange a WHATNOT meeting there, it could be an extraordinary one or we can try to move one of the usual ones to fit into the hackfest days/times; that would give more people the chance to see how these meetings happen; WDYT?
if you feel it's a good idea, should we fill an issue at https://github.com/whatwg/html/issues/ to start organizing it?
Are we having trouble with the HTML checker? I have a build failure in https://github.com/whatwg/fullscreen/actions/runs/25492816064/job/74805083038?pr=232
foolip: yes, see above.

Am I correct in thinking that a request's "destination" does not get copied over when you perform `new Request(fetchEvent.request)` in a Service Worker? From reading https://fetch.spec.whatwg.org/#dom-request I think that's right, but that seemed odd so I can't tell if I'm missing something.
content.formatted_body: Am I correct in thinking that a request's "destination" does not get copied over when you perform new Request(fetchEvent.request) in a Service Worker? From reading https://fetch.spec.whatwg.org/#dom-request I think that's right, but that seemed odd so I can't tell if I'm missing something.

content.body: Yes, we can't let the service worker pretend what request it's making.
Wait sorry can you clarify "pretend what request it's making"? Sec-Fetch-Dest derived from destination is copied over (just not exposed to JS), right
* Wait sorry can you clarify "pretend what request it's making"? Sec-Fetch-Dest derived from destination is copied over (just not exposed to JS), right?

m.new_content.body: Wait sorry can you clarify "pretend what request it's making"? Sec-Fetch-Dest derived from destination is copied over (just not exposed to JS), right?

content.body: Ah I guess that's OK since they'll get re-generated anyways when the SW re-fetches the new request? (Man, service workers are complicated.)
Sec-Fetch-Dest is always computed from scratch for each HTTP request

Right, so as long as we strip the request's destination concept member when we copy requests, we ensure that Sec-Fetch-Dest is effectively "wiped" if the SW tries to copy/fetch the request on its own. Is that right?

* Right, so as long as we strip the request's destination concept member when we copy requests, we ensure that Sec-Fetch-Dest is effectively "wiped" (or at least doesn't retain its old privileges) if the SW tries to copy/fetch the request on its own. Is that right?

m.new_content.body: Right, so as long as we strip the request's destination concept member when we copy requests, we ensure that Sec-Fetch-Dest is effectively "wiped" (or at least doesn't retain its old privileges) if the SW tries to copy/fetch the request on its own. Is that right?

content.body: Yea all the service workers fetch() requests are regular fetches because you can cache them/share them/use them for any destination later on

So the original fetch event's request retains its `destination`, and can be forwarded straight to `fetch()` and its `Sec-Fetch-Dest` will be generated accordingly, in a way that no script-constructed Request could, right? But that changes right when you copy the original request into a new Request—"destination" is lost and therefore you can't preserve `Sec-Fetch-Dest` etc. Is my mental model of this right?
content.formatted_body: So the original fetch event's request retains its destination, and can be forwarded straight to fetch() and its Sec-Fetch-Dest will be generated accordingly, in a way that no script-constructed Request could, right? But that changes right when you copy the original request into a new Request—"destination" is lost and therefore you can't preserve Sec-Fetch-Dest etc. Is my mental model of this right?

content.body: When you call `fetch()` I think `destination` will be reset.
content.formatted_body: When you call fetch() I think destination will be reset.

content.body: Ah I think you're right, because fetch() just delegate to the Request ctor right away, which doesn't carry over destination.
* Ah I think you're right, because fetch() just delegates to the Request ctor right away, which doesn't carry over destination.

m.new_content.body: Ah I think you're right, because fetch() just delegates to the Request ctor right away, which doesn't carry over destination.

content.body: * Ah I think you're right, because fetch() delegates to the Request ctor right away, which doesn't carry over destination.

m.new_content.body: Ah I think you're right, because fetch() delegates to the Request ctor right away, which doesn't carry over destination.

content.body: right, it's a rule of thumb that you can't use the same request for two different fetches. it's always cloned at some point

Hmm, I'm not sure that's the right mental model. You need to clone a request if you want to reuse it because we want the developer to be aware of the allocation cost. That's why bodies are consumed and not reusable anyway.
Maybe, but it was so far a useful mental model for me to remember how some things work especially around the fetch/sw integration. it's never the same request object
* Maybe, but it was so far a useful mental model for me to remember how some things work especially around the fetch/sw integration. it's never the same internal request object

m.new_content.body: Maybe, but it was so far a useful mental model for me to remember how some things work especially around the fetch/sw integration. it's never the same internal request object
